prompt:
Two complaints about the current ETRO essay. The article abuses bold font.
There's also not a clear separation between sections, somehow, or the
hierarchy not visually clean. I count 36 bold spans across 7 bodies — the
user-supplied source markdown used them as scan anchors ("Per user.",
"Scaffolding.", etc.). Fix it.
response:
I now have the full picture. The diagnosis is clear and complete. Here is the structured response:

---

## Diagnosis

The issue is **two simultaneous failures at two different layers** — one in the CMS data (Lexical content), one in the CSS renderer. The user is reporting them as a single UX problem ("hierarchy not visually clean"), which they are — but they have distinct root causes.

### Failure 1 — Bold abuse (CAP-030, content layer)

The Evolution Timeline in `docs/content/projects/etro-framework.md` records this fix as completed on **2026-04-17**, with the Lexical tree walk confirming 0 `FORMAT_BOLD` nodes. But the user is seeing it again today. There is one known cause for this: the AGENTS.md guardrail (EAP-087) exists precisely because previous sessions modified the seed route without calling the endpoint, leaving the CMS untouched. The documented resolution path is:

1. Strip all 36 `FORMAT_BOLD` nodes from the 7 richText bodies in `src/app/api/update-etro/route.ts`
2. Call `POST /api/update-etro` to push the clean Lexical tree to Payload

### Failure 2 — Section hierarchy collapse (FB-143, design layer)

`voice-style.md` §13.1 is explicit: **bold is only for section headings and impact metrics.** When body text carries `font-weight: 700` spans at the same scale as the H2 section heading (`heading-2` mixin = semibold 600), the weight contrast between heading and body collapses. The H2 at 600 and the body bold at 700 produce a visual hierarchy where the bold *inside* a paragraph reads heavier than the structural heading *above* it — exactly what the user described as "hierarchy not visually clean."

---

## Root Cause

**CAP-030 (Inline Bold Imported From Source Markdown)** — the CFB-035 materialization pass preserved every `**bold**` span from the user's raw markdown source (36 total across 7 bodies) as scan-anchor typography: "Per user.", "Scaffolding.", "Full ETRO slows adoption." etc. These were markdown reading conventions, not semantic emphasis. Inline bold at weight 700 in the body register competes directly with the semibold H2 section heading at weight 600, because the heading is heavier by role but lighter by value — visually overridden by body-level bold noise. Section rhythm collapses from within each paragraph.

The secondary cause for the hierarchy complaint is **no browser verification was performed** after the documented 2026-04-17 fix (EAP-087 / ENG-149 pattern: endpoint never called after route modification).

---

## Fix

### Step 1 — Strip all bold from the ETRO seed route

**File:** `src/app/api/update-etro/route.ts`

In every richText body across all 7 feature sections, find any text node with `format` including `FORMAT_BOLD` (Lexical bit flag `1`) and clear it to `0`. The conversion rules, which match what the ETRO dossier already specifies:

| Source bold type | Treatment |
|---|---|
| Scan-anchor labels ("Per user.", "Scaffolding.", "Structure:", "Migration:") | Convert to colon-prefixed plain paragraph start: `Per user:` at weight 400 |
| Sentence-level emphasis (e.g. "Full ETRO slows adoption.") | Strip bold, render as plain body text — the sentence content carries the emphasis |
| Tonal emphasis (single meaningful words) | Convert `**word**` → `*word*` (italic, not bold) — italic is not banned |
| Any `### heading` inside a `bodyMarkdown` block | Remove entirely — `lexicalToHtml` silently drops heading nodes inside richText roots |

Concretely, across all 7 section `bodyRichText` objects in the seed route, the Lexical paragraph node structure must produce zero nodes matching this predicate:

```typescript
// Remove this pattern from every text node in every richText body
node.type === 'text' && (node.format & 1) !== 0  // FORMAT_BOLD flag
```

The corrected Lexical text nodes look like:

```typescript
// ❌ Before — bold scan anchor
{
  type: 'text',
  text: 'Per user.',
  format: 1,  // FORMAT_BOLD
  version: 1,
}

// ✅ After — colon-prefixed plain start
{
  type: 'text',
  text: 'Per user: ',
  format: 0,  // no formatting
  version: 1,
}
```

For emphasis cases that genuinely need contrast, use italic (format bit `2`):

```typescript
// ✅ Tonal emphasis → italic only
{
  type: 'text',
  text: 'right kind',
  format: 2,  // FORMAT_ITALIC — not banned
  version: 1,
}
```

### Step 2 — Call the endpoint (EAP-087 mandatory)

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:4000/api/update-etro
```

Verify the response contains `"action": "updated"` and all 7 headings and 15 blocks are present. Then open `/work/etro-framework` in the browser and confirm:

- No `<strong>` or `<b>` tags appear in the rendered section bodies
- The H2 section headings (semibold 600) read visually heavier than all body text
- Scan-anchor labels appear as plain colon-prefixed paragraph openers

### Step 3 — CSS: reinforce section boundary spacing (design layer)

If the hierarchy still reads flat after the bold strip (the H2 alone at 600 may not be enough separation against dense prose), the correct fix is **spacing before section headings**, not bold body text. In `page.module.scss` (`.layoutEssay`):

```scss
// In the essay section body renderer — each section block
.sectionHeading {
  // Existing heading-2 mixin provides 36px / semibold-600
  // Add top margin to visually open a clear gap from the body above
  margin-top: $portfolio-spacer-4x;   // 32px — section boundary air
  margin-bottom: $portfolio-spacer-1x; // 8px  — heading-to-body tightness
}
```

This re-establishes the **three-tier reading rhythm** the essay requires:

```
[ Section body prose — weight 400, 18px ]
                                          ← 32px gap (new boundary signal)
[ H2 Section heading — weight 600, 36px ]
                                          ← 8px gap (heading binds to its body)
[ Section body prose — weight 400, 18px ]
```

Without this spacing, the visual boundary relies entirely on font size contrast. With 36 bold body spans competing, that contrast disappears. Once the spans are gone, spacing alone carries the boundary cleanly.

---

## Principle

> **Bold in prose is a structural signal, not an emphasis tool.** (CAP-030 / `voice-style.md` §13.1)

In a typographic system where H2 headings sit at `semibold-600` and body text at `regular-400`, inline `bold-700` body spans invert the visual hierarchy: the body becomes heavier than the heading. There is no per-span patch that fixes this — the rule is categorical. Body text uses weight 400. Impact metrics may use weight 500 (`body-base-medium`). Headings own 600 and 700. This is the same principle IBM Carbon uses in the weight matrix: **each weight level maps to exactly one semantic role** and those roles do not overlap.

When source markdown arrives with `**bold**` scan anchors — a reading convention from document-style writing, not prose — strip them all before materializing into Lexical. Convert structural labels to colon-prefixed plain openers. Convert tonal emphasis to italic. The sentence structure and word choice carry the weight. Never delegate emphasis to a CSS weight override inside paragraph prose.
